draft.introduction: Luke’s nativity sets Jesus’ birth within Roman imperial history—the census of Augustus, the manger in Bethlehem, the angels announcing peace. It is the most literarily crafted birth narrative in the NT.

translation.text: In those days a decree went out from Caesar Augustus that all the world should be registered. And Joseph also went up from Galilee, from the city of Nazareth, to Judea, to the city of David, which is called Bethlehem. And she gave birth to her firstborn son and wrapped him in swaddling cloths and laid him in a manger, because there was no place for them in the inn. And the angel said to them, “Fear not, for behold, I bring you good news of great joy. For unto you is born this day in the city of David a Savior, who is Christ the Lord.”
translation.original_text: ἐγένετο δὲ ἐν ταῖς ἡμέραις ἐκείναις ἐξῆλθεν δόγμα παρὰ Καίσαρος Αὐγούστου

translator_notes.note: The triple title “Savior, Christ, Lord” (σωτήρ ὅς ἐστιν χριστὸς κύριος) directly challenges imperial titles. Augustus was called “savior” and “lord”; Luke’s angel transfers these titles to an infant in a manger.
